text.title: api-monitoring-health-checks Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules grounding API Monitoring — Health Checks. Each rule pairs a binary check + sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Liveness endpoint (/healthz) MUST check ONLY that the process is alive. It MUST NOT call any downstream service (database, Redis, external API). It returns 200 with a JSON body containing status and timestamp.
rule.rationale: If liveness calls downstream and that downstream is slow, Kubernetes restarts the healthy process. This turns a dependency outage into a cascading restart loop that takes down your service even though the process itself was fine.

rule.statement: Readiness endpoint (/readyz) MUST check all critical dependencies (database, cache, external APIs). It MUST return 503 with the failing check names in the response body when any dependency is unreachable. It MUST never trigger a container restart — wire to readiness probes only, never liveness.
rule.rationale: Readiness gates traffic. When readiness fails, the orchestrator removes the pod from load-balancer rotation; the process stays running and can recover when the dependency comes back.

rule.statement: External dependency URLs in readiness probes MUST be from the vendor's documented health/status contract (e.g., Stripe: https://status.stripe.com/api/v2/status.json) or an in-house canary endpoint. Invented URLs like https://api.stripe.com/health are fictional; the probe will always fail.
rule.rationale: AI agents and copy-paste culture produce hallucinated vendor health URLs constantly. Agents frequently use README's https://api.stripe.com/health — that URL does not exist. Always verify against vendor docs before wiring.

rule.statement: Probe handlers MUST respond in ≤200ms p99 with no downstream calls in `/healthz`. `/readyz` may make bounded downstream calls (single timeout ≤500ms total).
rule.rationale: Kubernetes default probe period is 10s. A slow probe pads the pod restart cycle and produces false unready events under load. Liveness must be cheap; readiness may be bounded but never unbounded.

rule.statement: Use Kubernetes `startupProbe` (or equivalent) to defer liveness/readiness for slow-starting apps; liveness should not kill a pod that has not yet reached steady state.

rationale.source: Kubernetes startupProbe docs; SRE startup patterns

rule.rationale: Liveness probes that fire before the app has loaded its caches cause restart storms in cold starts. startupProbe gives a bounded grace period.

rule.statement: Skip this methodology when the runtime has no orchestrator (single-host CRON job, lambda) and no LB-driven traffic shaping.
rule.rationale: Probes only matter when something acts on them (k8s, LB, mesh). Lambda/CRON don't consume probes.
